channel.title: Bioinformatics on Yige Wu, PhD
channel.description: Recent content in Bioinformatics on Yige Wu, PhD

item.title: The Trap of 1D Target Screening: Why Abundance Doesn't Predict Toxicity
item.description:
The Challenge

During a recent technical discussion regarding on-target, off-tumor toxicity, we evaluated ERBB2 (HER2) as a case study. The standard practice in bioinformatics is to screen out targets with high normal-tissue expression using databases like GTEx (for RNA) or the Human Protein Atlas (for Protein).

However, looking at the GTEx RNA-seq data, ERBB2 is highly abundant in the gastrointestinal tract (esophagus) and urinary bladder. Yet, the most famous, dose-limiting toxicities for HER2-targeted therapies (like Trastuzumab) are Cardiotoxicity and Interstitial Lung Disease (ILD)—tissues with comparatively unremarkable baseline RNA expression.

item.title: Forensics on the Filesystem: Identifying Unannotated FASTQ Data
item.description:
The Challenge

In a recent technical discussion, I tackled a practical data hygiene problem: You are handed a raw, unannotated FASTQ file with no metadata. Is it RNA-seq (transcriptomic) or DNA-seq (genomic)?

Running a full alignment on a multi-gigabyte file just to check the data type is computationally wasteful. We need a heuristic to determine the identity quickly.

The Biological Insight

The key difference lies in the physical origins of the fragments.
